framing_notes: The premise that identification has 'risen materially and continuously' is a hypothesis to test, not a fact; research must separate a real change in underlying prevalence from changes in diagnostic practice, category definitions, access to assessment, and administrative or financial incentives.

The five human-proposed response domains (specialist workforce, mainstream teaching methods, finance/governance/placement, school-parent communication, international models) are areas to examine, not approved solutions; this brief deliberately stops short of proposing interventions.

Category boundaries matter: SEN, BTMN, autism, behavioural difficulty, and safety incidents are distinct and must not be conflated when interpreting trends or reporting parent and teacher experience, since they carry different causes, rights, and provision implications.

The 'parental flight' and 'institutional sorting' claims are causal hypotheses — reviewers should note the research must establish whether mobility occurs, who can exercise it, and its compositional effects before any equity conclusion is drawn.

A central value tension to keep visible: the interests of children identified with SEN, of other children in the classroom, of teachers, and of parents exercising school choice may diverge, and any later option space will have to make these trade-offs explicit rather than assume a single 'right' balance.

The link between identification/provision and inequality is the submitter's core concern; the brief preserves it as a connected chain (capacity → classroom experience → parental choice → sorting → inequality) so research does not examine these elements in isolation.
Reviewers should confirm whether the temporal window ('past decade'), the education levels, and the school sectors implied here match the intended scope before research begins.
learning_goals: Establish whether recorded SEN (and related BTMN, autism, and behavioural) identification has changed materially over the past decade, distinguishing prevalence, measurement, definitional, access, and incentive-related explanations for any measured trend.

Characterise current specialist workforce and support-service capacity (including travelling services) and methodological capacity in mainstream settings, and assess whether gaps constrain adequate support or shift burden onto mainstream teachers.
Document parent and teacher classroom experience of integration without conflating SEN, BTMN, autism, behavioural difficulty, and safety incidents.

Test whether selective parental mobility ('flight') occurs in response to identification and integration, including who is able to move, which school sectors absorb movers, and how this changes school composition.

Trace the links between identification, provision, parental school choice, institutional sorting, and educational inequality, and connect these to children's outcomes, classroom functioning, rights, and inclusion.

Map the finance, governance, placement, and accountability arrangements that shape identification and provision, and identify which value choices and trade-offs any policy response would have to confront.
candidate.problem_statement: Observers report that a growing share of children in Hungary are being formally identified as having special educational needs (SEN), alongside related categories such as BTMN (learning and behavioural integration difficulties), autism, and behavioural or safety concerns. Whether such a rise has actually occurred, how large and continuous it is, and what it means are all open questions that research must verify rather than assume. A measured change in recorded identification could reflect several distinct and possibly overlapping drivers: real changes in prevalence (biological or environmental), changes in diagnostic practice or category definitions, changes in access to assessment, or incentives embedded in finance, placement, and accountability rules. The submitter's underlying concern connects this identification trend to whether the specialist workforce and support methods can meet demand, how mainstream classrooms and teachers experience integration, whether parents move children between schools or classes in response, and whether such mobility reshapes school composition in ways that deepen educational inequality. This brief bounds the problem so later research can test these premises and map — without prejudging — where policy attention might be warranted. It does not yet answer the question or endorse any response.
candidate.public_question: More children are being identified as having special educational needs — what should Hungary do?
candidate.scope: In scope: children in Hungarian public education identified with SEN and adjacent categories (BTMN, autism, behavioural and safety concerns), the identification and assessment process, specialist and mainstream support capacity, classroom experience, parental school choice and mobility, institutional sorting, and educational inequality — examined as interconnected phenomena. The brief covers verifying the claimed identification trend and its possible drivers, and mapping the decision-relevant terrain of capacity, experience, choice, sorting, and inequality. Out of scope for this brief: proposing or evaluating specific interventions, scenarios, or preferred outcomes; recommending governance or funding changes; clinical diagnostic protocol design; and any assertion that the trend, its causes, or the hypothesised mechanisms are established fact. International inclusive models are in scope only as comparative evidence subject to explicit Hungarian transferability tests, not as endorsed solutions.
candidate.title: Rising Special Educational Needs Identification in Hungary: Framing the Policy Problem
